Rezvan Explains: Classical Machine Learning

Date: January 20, 2025
Last modified: May 27, 2025
50 min read

As of writing this, last night as I was going to sleep, I was lying in bed and thought about the future. After some rambling I came up with this (hopefully) series, Rezvan Explains. I took a course called machine learning during my exchange semester @ CityUHK taught by Kede Ma (wonderful professor and I highly recommend taking the course). This post is very inspired by his course but, I want to add my touch on the topic.

My goal for this part is to build your intuition when it comes to machine learning. I believe most people getting started with AI nowadays don’t want to understand the foundations modern deep learning is built upon.

Preqrequisites and Notation

This post will assume that you know about:

  • Linear Algebra
  • Multivariable and Matrix Calculus
  • Probability and Statistics

If you don’t feel comfortable with these topics, read up and come back when ready.

These are the notations that we will use, there is no need to go through them the first time you’re reading this, it’s meant as a reference.

SymbolMeaning
MMMNumber of Examples in Dataset
NNNNumber of Features in Training Sample
D\mathcal{D}DDataset of MMM Examples
x\mathbf{x}xNNN-dimensional Feature Vector
yyyClass Label
x(i)\mathbf{x}^{(i)}x(i)iii-th Feature Vector in Dataset in D\mathcal{D}D
xj(i)\mathbf{x}^{(i)}_jxj(i)​jjj-th Feature in iii-th Feature Vector in D\mathcal{D}D
y(i)y^{(i)}y(i)iii-th Class Label in Dataset in D\mathcal{D}D corresponding to x(i)\mathbf{x}^{(i)}x(i)
X\mathbf{X}XInput Space
Y\mathbf{Y}YOutput Space
∥Y∥\| \mathbf{Y} \|∥Y∥Number of Classes in Output Space

What, Why, and How?

There is one fundamental flaw I’ve seen with every machine learning course I’ve taken. Machine learning is a study of statistical algorithms, i.e., statistics and machine learning are very closely related fields. But the distinct difference is in their principal goal.

Statistics VS. Machine Learning

Generally in machine learning we want to find generalizable predictive patterns. 1 While this might not be exclusive to machine learning (a large part of statistics is also about prediction), in machine learning, it is the main goal.

I’m not going into the history of machine learning as a field and its evolution, but if it is one thing I want you to take away from this post, it is that machine learning fundamentally relies on data. I can’t stress this enough, a lot of my fellow peers never got this intuition at the start of our courses, and therefore everything during the semester seemed like black magic. But in the end everything comes down to data.

Humble Beginnings

Okay I lied, a bit of history won’t hurt. The name machine learning implies that a machine is learning (duh). I think the machine part is quite obvious (a computer), but learning is a bit more abstract.

A lot of people have defined learning in varying ways and for different domains, let’s take a look:

  • Behaviorism (Skinner)

    • Learning is a long-term change in behavior due to experience. 2

  • Cognitivism (Gestalt School)

    • Learning is an internal mental process that integrates new information into established mental frameworks and updates those frameworks over time. 3

  • Connectionism (Hebbian Learning)

    • Learning is a physical process in which neurons join by developing the synapses between them. 4

We can see that in an abstract sense, learning is taking some input and changing some internal state based on that input. The last one is an outlier, but remember this for later.

Different Types of Learning

Depending on the type of data we have but also what we want to achieve, the learning process needs to be different.

In this post, we will focus on supervised learning and unsupervised learning.

Let’s define supervised learning in mathematical terms.

Supervised Learning

In supervised learning, we have a dataset consisting of well-defined input-output pairs. The task is to find a (good) generalized mapping from an arbitrary input to an output.

Mathematically we define this as,

f:X↦Y,\begin{equation} f : \mathbf{X} \mapsto \mathbf{Y}, \end{equation}f:X↦Y,​​

where X\mathbf{X}X is the input space and Y\mathbf{Y}Y is the output space, we want to find the best function fff that maps X\mathbf{X}X to Y\mathbf{Y}Y.

The Classification Task

The best way to understand supervised learning is through the classification task and Fisher’s Iris dataset. 5

In Fisher’s Iris dataset we have 150 samples of input-output pairs. The input is a vector with four features — sepal length, sepal width, petal length, petal width — the output is the species of the iris flower.

As described above, our input and output spaces are,

X={Sepal Length,Sepal Width,Petal Length,Petal Width}Y={Setosa,Versicolor,Virginica}\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathbf{X} & = \{ \text{Sepal Length}, \text{Sepal Width}, \text{Petal Length}, \text{Petal Width} \} \newline \mathbf{Y} & = \{ \text{Setosa}, \text{Versicolor}, \text{Virginica} \} \end{aligned} \end{equation}XY​={Sepal Length,Sepal Width,Petal Length,Petal Width}={Setosa,Versicolor,Virginica}​​​

So given a vector with four real numbers (where each number describes a feature of the iris flower), we want to predict a number that corresponds to the species of the iris flower.

Thus, the proper mathematical definition of the classification task is,

Definition: Definition: The Classification Task

Given a feature vector x∈X=RN\mathbf{x} \in \mathbf{X} = \mathbb{R}^Nx∈X=RN that describes an object that belongs to one of CCC classes from the set Y={1,2,…,C}\mathbf{Y} = \{ 1, 2, \ldots, C \}Y={1,2,…,C}, predict the class label y∈Yy \in \mathbf{Y}y∈Y.

The Classification Learning Problem

Now that we have defined the classification task, let’s define the classification learning problem,

Definition: Definition: The Classification Learning Problem

Given a dataset of example pairs D={(x(i),y(i)),i=1,…,M}\mathcal{D} = \{ (\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}), i = 1, \ldots, M \}D={(x(i),y(i)),i=1,…,M} where x(i)∈X=RN\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathbf{X} = \mathbb{R}^Nx(i)∈X=RN is a feature vector and y(i)∈Y={1,…,C}y^{(i)} \in \mathbf{Y} = \{1, \ldots, C\}y(i)∈Y={1,…,C} is the class label, learn a function,

f:RN↦Yf: \mathbb{R}^N \mapsto \mathbf{Y}f:RN↦Y

that accurately predicts the class label yyy for any feature vector x\mathbf{x}x.

Metrics

To determine how well our function fff is doing, we need to define some (primal) metrics.

Firstly, let’s define the indicator function,

I[A]={1,if A is true,0,otherwise.\begin{equation} \mathbb{I}[A] = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } A \text{ is true}, \newline 0, & \text{otherwise}. \end{cases} \end{equation}I[A]={1,0,​if A is true,otherwise.​​​

The classification error is then defined as,

Definition: Definition: The Classification Error

Given a dataset of example pairs D={(x(i),y(i)),i=1,…,M}\mathcal{D} = \{ (\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}), i = 1, \ldots, M \}D={(x(i),y(i)),i=1,…,M} and a function f:RN↦Yf: \mathbb{R}^N \mapsto \mathbf{Y}f:RN↦Y, the classification error of fff on D\mathcal{D}D is defined as,

Error(f,D)=1M∑i=1MI[f(x(i))≠y(i)].\begin{equation} \text{Error}(f, \mathcal{D}) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \mathbb{I}[f(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) \neq y^{(i)}]. \end{equation}Error(f,D)=M1​i=1∑M​I[f(x(i))=y(i)].​​

I.e., the classification error is the fraction of examples in the dataset that are misclassified by the function fff.

The classification accuracy is then defined as,

Definition: Definition: The Classification Accuracy

Given a dataset of example pairs D={(x(i),y(i)),i=1,…,M}\mathcal{D} = \{ (\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}), i = 1, \ldots, M \}D={(x(i),y(i)),i=1,…,M} and a function f:RN↦Yf: \mathbb{R}^N \mapsto \mathbf{Y}f:RN↦Y, the classification accuracy of fff on D\mathcal{D}D is defined as,

Accuracy(f,D)=1M∑i=1MI[f(x(i))=y(i)]=1−Error(f,D).\begin{equation} \text{Accuracy}(f, \mathcal{D}) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \mathbb{I}[f(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) = y^{(i)}] = 1 - \text{Error}(f, \mathcal{D}). \end{equation}Accuracy(f,D)=M1​i=1∑M​I[f(x(i))=y(i)]=1−Error(f,D).​​

Different Approaches

But how do we solve the classification learning problem? We will go through different ways of approaching this problem.

But I want to start of with the probabilistic approach first.

Generative Models

We know that our dataset D\mathcal{D}D consists of input-output pairs (x(i),y(i))(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})(x(i),y(i)). In the real dataset, we have a joint distribution p(x,y)p(\mathbf{x}, y)p(x,y), this describes the probability of observing the pair (x,y)(\mathbf{x}, y)(x,y).

We can learn this joint distribution from the dataset. We know from Bayes’ Rule that,

p(x,y)=p(y)p(x∣y).\begin{equation} p(\mathbf{x}, y) = p(y) p(\mathbf{x} | y). \end{equation}p(x,y)=p(y)p(x∣y).​​

These two terms are very important, let’s define them properly.

The first term p(y)p(y)p(y) is often called the prior distribution of the classes. It describes how frequently each class occurs in nature (or in the dataset).

The second term p(x∣y)p(\mathbf{x} | y)p(x∣y) is called the class-conditional distribution. This distribution describes how the features x\mathbf{x}x are distributed for different classes yyy.

To remind you, the classification task is to predict the class label yyy given a feature vector x\mathbf{x}x.

So again, by Bayes’s Rule we know that,

p(y∣x)=p(x,y)p(x)=p(y)p(x∣y)p(x).\begin{equation} p(y | \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x}, y)}{p(\mathbf{x})} = \frac{p(y) p(\mathbf{x} | y)}{p(\mathbf{x})}. \end{equation}p(y∣x)=p(x)p(x,y)​=p(x)p(y)p(x∣y)​.​​

So for the classification task, we want to choose the best prediction.

Let’s clarify the difference between the max⁡\maxmax and arg⁡max⁡\arg \maxargmax operators. The max⁡\maxmax returns the maximum value of a function, while the arg⁡max⁡\arg \maxargmax returns the argument that gives the maximum value.

Or in mathematical terms,

max⁡xf(x)={f(x)∣x∈D∧∀y∈D,f(y)≤f(x)}\begin{equation} \max_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x}) = \{f(\mathbf{x}) | \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{D} \land \forall \mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{D}, f(\mathbf{y}) \leq f(\mathbf{x})\} \end{equation}xmax​f(x)={f(x)∣x∈D∧∀y∈D,f(y)≤f(x)}​​ arg⁡max⁡xf(x)={x∣x∈D∧∀y∈D,f(y)≤f(x)}\begin{equation} \underset{\mathbf{x}}{\arg\max} f(\mathbf{x}) = \{\mathbf{x} | \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{D} \land \forall \mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{D}, f(\mathbf{y}) \leq f(\mathbf{x})\} \end{equation}xargmax​f(x)={x∣x∈D∧∀y∈D,f(y)≤f(x)}​​

Therefore, the best prediction for the classification task is,

y^=arg⁡max⁡y∈Yp(y∣x)=arg⁡max⁡y∈Yp(y)p(x∣y)p(x).\begin{equation} \hat{y} = \arg \max_{y \in \mathbf{Y}} p(y | \mathbf{x}) = \arg \max_{y \in \mathbf{Y}} \frac{p(y) p(\mathbf{x} | y)}{p(\mathbf{x})}. \end{equation}y^​=argy∈Ymax​p(y∣x)=argy∈Ymax​p(x)p(y)p(x∣y)​.​​

If we are looking to maximize the fraction, we can ignore the denominator p(x)p(\mathbf{x})p(x) since we know that it is non-negative and class-independent.

y^=arg⁡max⁡y∈Yp(y)p(x∣y).\begin{equation} \hat{y} = \arg \max_{y \in \mathbf{Y}} p(y) p(\mathbf{x} | y). \end{equation}y^​=argy∈Ymax​p(y)p(x∣y).​​

So if we can estimate the prior distribution p(y)p(y)p(y) and the class-conditional distribution p(x∣y)p(\mathbf{x} | y)p(x∣y), we can predict the class label yyy for any feature vector x\mathbf{x}x.

Let’s start with the prior distribution p(y)p(y)p(y), since it is the easiest to learn. From our dataset D={(x(i),y(i)),i=1,…,M}\mathcal{D} = \{ (\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}), i = 1, \ldots, M \}D={(x(i),y(i)),i=1,…,M}, with well-defined input-output pairs we simply count how often each class label yyy appears,

p(y=c)≈∑i=1MI[y(i)=c]M.\begin{equation} p(y = c) \approx \frac{\sum_{i = 1}^M \mathbb{I}[y^{(i)} = c]}{M}. \end{equation}p(y=c)≈M∑i=1M​I[y(i)=c]​.​​

Note, that this is the simplest case if yyy only captures one class, it is possible that for a different task that yyy is a vector of classes (e.g., multi-label classification).

The class-conditional distribution p(x∣y)p(\mathbf{x} | y)p(x∣y) is a bit more tricky. How do we model this distribution?

By looking at Figure 1, we see that it is a bit noisy, but we can see some Gaussian structure. Let’s assume a probability model for the class conditional p(x∣y)p(\mathbf{x} | y)p(x∣y), in our case Gaussian.

Bayes Optimal Classifier

So, each class is modeled as a separate Gaussian distribution of the feature values, or in other words,

p(x∣y)=12πσc2e−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2\begin{equation} p(\mathbf{x} | y) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_{c}^2}} e^{-\frac{1}{2 \sigma_{c}^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_{c}\right)^2} \end{equation}p(x∣y)=2πσc2​​1​e−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2​​

Each class has its own mean and variance parameters (μc\mu_{c}μc​ and σc2\sigma_{c}^2σc2​). But what’s considered the “best” estimates, and how do we find them?

Maximum Likelihood Estimate

We want our model to be as close to the true distribution as possible. Meaning that our (already) observed data should be the most probable under our model.

The likelihood function measures how well a model explains the observed data given the model parameters 6.

Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE) is a method for estimating said parameters by maximizing the likelihood function.

Note, there are more sophisticated methods (e.g., Bayesian estimation, Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation, etc.), but MLE has very nice properties and is good enough for most cases.

In our case, the likelihood function is defined as,

L(μc,σc2)=∏i=1Mc12πσc2e−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2,\begin{equation} L(\mu_c, \sigma_c^2) = \prod_{i=1}^{M_c} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_{c}^2}} e^{-\frac{1}{2 \sigma_{c}^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_{c}\right)^2}, \end{equation}L(μc​,σc2​)=i=1∏Mc​​2πσc2​​1​e−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2,​​

where McM_cMc​ is the number of samples in class ccc, since the parameters are different for each class.

Thus, our MLE estimates are,

(μc^,σc2^)=arg⁡max⁡μc,σc2∏i=1Mc12πσc2e−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2.\begin{equation} (\hat{\mu_c}, \hat{\sigma_c^2}) = \underset{\mu_c, \sigma_c^2}{\arg\max} \prod_{i=1}^{M_c} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_{c}^2}} e^{-\frac{1}{2 \sigma_{c}^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_{c}\right)^2}. \end{equation}(μc​^​,σc2​^​)=μc​,σc2​argmax​i=1∏Mc​​2πσc2​​1​e−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2.​​

Let’s use a little trick to avoid the product notation. The logarithm is a monotonic function, meaning that the order of the values doesn’t change. In other words, the maximum value before applying the logarithm is still the maximum value after applying the logarithm (just the scale changes).

Thus, taking the MLE of the log-likelihood function is equivalent to taking the MLE of the likelihood function.

(μc^,σc2^)=arg⁡max⁡μc,σc2log⁡[∏i=1Mc12πσc2e−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2].\begin{equation} (\hat{\mu_c}, \hat{\sigma_c^2}) = \underset{\mu_c, \sigma_c^2}{\arg\max} \log \left[\prod_{i=1}^{M_c} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_{c}^2}} e^{-\frac{1}{2 \sigma_{c}^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_{c}\right)^2}\right]. \end{equation}(μc​^​,σc2​^​)=μc​,σc2​argmax​log[i=1∏Mc​​2πσc2​​1​e−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2].​​

Now, the product becomes a sum (since the logarithm of a product is the sum of the logarithms).

(μc^,σc2^)=arg⁡max⁡μc,σc2∑i=1Mclog⁡[12πσc2e−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2].\begin{equation} (\hat{\mu_c}, \hat{\sigma_c^2}) = \underset{\mu_c, \sigma_c^2}{\arg\max} \sum_{i=1}^{M_c} \log \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_{c}^2}} e^{-\frac{1}{2 \sigma_{c}^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_{c}\right)^2}\right]. \end{equation}(μc​^​,σc2​^​)=μc​,σc2​argmax​i=1∑Mc​​log[2πσc2​​1​e−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2].​​

Now, I am going to simplify the expression step-by-step, at some steps I will have a ∣⋅\big\rvert \cdot​⋅ to indicate what operation we perform at that step.

log⁡[12πσc2e−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2]=log⁡[12πσc2]+log⁡[e−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2] ∣log⁡(ab)=log⁡(a)+log⁡(b)=log⁡(1)−log⁡(2πσc2)+log⁡[e−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2] ∣log⁡(a/b)=log⁡(a)−log⁡(b)=−log⁡(2πσc2)+log⁡[e−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2] ∣log⁡(1)=0=−log⁡(2πσc2)−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2 ∣log⁡(ea)=a\begin{aligned} \log \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_{c}^2}} e^{-\frac{1}{2 \sigma_{c}^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_{c}\right)^2}\right] &= \log \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_{c}^2}}\right] + \log \left[e^{-\frac{1}{2 \sigma_{c}^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_{c}\right)^2}\right] \ \biggr\rvert \log(ab) = \log(a) + \log(b) \newline &= \log(1) - \log(\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_{c}^2}) + \log \left[e^{-\frac{1}{2 \sigma_{c}^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_{c}\right)^2}\right] \ \biggr\rvert \log(a/b) = \log(a) - \log(b) \newline &= -\log(\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_{c}^2}) + \log \left[e^{-\frac{1}{2 \sigma_{c}^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_{c}\right)^2}\right] \ \biggr\rvert \log(1) = 0 \newline &= -\log(\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_{c}^2}) -\frac{1}{2 \sigma_{c}^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_{c}\right)^2 \ \biggr\rvert \log(e^a) = a \newline \end{aligned}log[2πσc2​​1​e−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2]​=log[2πσc2​​1​]+log[e−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2] ​log(ab)=log(a)+log(b)=log(1)−log(2πσc2​​)+log[e−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2] ​log(a/b)=log(a)−log(b)=−log(2πσc2​​)+log[e−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2] ​log(1)=0=−log(2πσc2​​)−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2 ​log(ea)=a​

So, our MLE estimates are,

(μc^,σc2^)=arg⁡max⁡μc,σc2∑i=1Mc[−log⁡(2πσc2)−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2].\begin{equation} (\hat{\mu_c}, \hat{\sigma_c^2}) = \underset{\mu_c, \sigma_c^2}{\arg\max} \sum_{i=1}^{M_c} \left[-\log(\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_{c}^2}) -\frac{1}{2 \sigma_{c}^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_{c}\right)^2\right]. \end{equation}(μc​^​,σc2​^​)=μc​,σc2​argmax​i=1∑Mc​​[−log(2πσc2​​)−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2].​​

To find the maximum, we need to take the derivative of the expression with respect to μc\mu_cμc​ and σc2\sigma_c^2σc2​ and set them to zero.

Let’s start with μc\mu_cμc​ since it’s easier.

∂∂μc∑[−log⁡(2πσc2)−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2]=0∑∂∂μc[−log⁡(2πσc2)⏟0−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2]=0∑1σc2(x(i)−μc)=0 ∣Chain Rule∑(x(i)−μc)=0 ∣⋅σc2∑x(i)−∑μc=0∑x(i)−Mcμc=0 ∣ Since μc doesn’t depend on iμc^=1Mc∑x(i)\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_c} \sum \left[-\log(\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_c^2}) -\frac{1}{2 \sigma_c^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_c\right)^2\right] &= 0 \newline \sum \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_c} \left[\underbrace{-\log(\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_c^2})}_0 -\frac{1}{2 \sigma_c^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_c\right)^2\right] &= 0 \newline \sum \frac{1}{\sigma_c^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_c\right) &= 0 \ \biggr\rvert \text{Chain Rule} \newline \sum \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_c\right) &= 0 \ \biggr\rvert \cdot \sigma_c^2 \newline \sum x^{(i)} - \sum \mu_c &= 0 \newline \sum x^{(i)} - M_c \mu_c &= 0 \ \biggr\rvert \text{ Since $\mu_c$ doesn't depend on $i$} \newline \hat{\mu_c} &= \boxed{\frac{1}{M_c} \sum x^{(i)}} \end{aligned} \end{equation}∂μc​∂​∑[−log(2πσc2​​)−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2]∑∂μc​∂​​0−log(2πσc2​​)​​−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2​∑σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)∑(x(i)−μc​)∑x(i)−∑μc​∑x(i)−Mc​μc​μc​^​​=0=0=0 ​Chain Rule=0 ​⋅σc2​=0=0 ​ Since μc​ doesn’t depend on i=Mc​1​∑x(i)​​​​

Now, let’s find σc2\sigma_c^2σc2​.

∂∂σc2∑[−log⁡(2πσc2)−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2]=0∑∂∂σc2[−log⁡(2πσc2)−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2]=0∑∂∂σc2[−log⁡((2πσc2)1/2)−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2]=0 ∣a=a1/2∑∂∂σc2[−12log⁡(2πσc2)−12σc2(x(i)−μc)2]=0 ∣log⁡(ab)=blog⁡(a)∑[−1212πσc2⋅2π+12σc4(x(i)−μc)2]=0∑[−12σc2+12σc4(x(i)−μc)2]=0∑[−1+1σc2(x(i)−μc)2]=0 ∣⋅2σc2−Mc+∑1σc2(x(i)−μc)2=0∑1σc2(x(i)−μc)2=Mc1σc2∑(x(i)−μc)2=Mc ∣Since σc2 doesn’t depend on iσc2^=1Mc∑(x(i)−μc^)2\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial}{\partial \sigma_c^2} \sum \left[-\log(\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_c^2}) -\frac{1}{2 \sigma_c^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_c\right)^2\right] &= 0 \newline \sum \frac{\partial}{\partial \sigma_c^2} \left[-\log(\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma_c^2}) -\frac{1}{2 \sigma_c^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_c\right)^2\right] &= 0 \newline \sum \frac{\partial}{\partial \sigma_c^2} \left[-\log(\left(2 \pi \sigma_c^2\right)^{1/2}) -\frac{1}{2 \sigma_c^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_c\right)^2\right] &= 0 \ \biggr\rvert \sqrt{a} = a^{1/2} \newline \sum \frac{\partial}{\partial \sigma_c^2} \left[-\frac{1}{2} \log(2 \pi \sigma_c^2) -\frac{1}{2 \sigma_c^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_c\right)^2\right] &= 0 \ \biggr\rvert \log(a^b) = b \log(a) \newline \sum \left[-\frac{1}{2} \frac{1}{2 \pi \sigma_c^2} \cdot 2 \pi + \frac{1}{2 \sigma_c^4} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_c\right)^2\right] &= 0 \newline \sum \left[-\frac{1}{2 \sigma_c^2} + \frac{1}{2 \sigma_c^4} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_c\right)^2\right] &= 0 \newline \sum \left[-1 + \frac{1}{\sigma_c^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_c\right)^2\right] &= 0 \ \biggr\rvert \cdot 2\sigma_c^2 \newline -M_c + \sum \frac{1}{\sigma_c^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_c\right)^2 &= 0 \newline \sum \frac{1}{\sigma_c^2} \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_c\right)^2 &= M_c \newline \frac{1}{\sigma_c^2} \sum \left(x^{(i)} - \mu_c\right)^2 &= M_c \ \biggr\rvert \text{Since $\sigma_c^2$ doesn't depend on $i$} \newline \hat{\sigma_c^2} &= \boxed{\frac{1}{M_c} \sum \left(x^{(i)} - \hat{\mu_c}\right)^2} \end{aligned} \end{equation}∂σc2​∂​∑[−log(2πσc2​​)−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2]∑∂σc2​∂​[−log(2πσc2​​)−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2]∑∂σc2​∂​[−log((2πσc2​)1/2)−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2]∑∂σc2​∂​[−21​log(2πσc2​)−2σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2]∑[−21​2πσc2​1​⋅2π+2σc4​1​(x(i)−μc​)2]∑[−2σc2​1​+2σc4​1​(x(i)−μc​)2]∑[−1+σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2]−Mc​+∑σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2∑σc2​1​(x(i)−μc​)2σc2​1​∑(x(i)−μc​)2σc2​^​​=0=0=0 ​a​=a1/2=0 ​log(ab)=blog(a)=0=0=0 ​⋅2σc2​=0=Mc​=Mc​ ​Since σc2​ doesn’t depend on i=Mc​1​∑(x(i)−μc​^​)2​​​​

In our example from Figure 1, if we plot the Gaussian estimates, we get the following plot,

We can see that Figure 2 indeed captures these distributions quite nicely.

Bayes Optimal Classifier Summary

Let’s summarize what we have learned about Bayes Optimal Classifier and how we can use the Bayesian Decision Rule to classify new data points.

Given an observation x\mathbf{x}x, we want to pick the class ccc with the highest posterior probability p(y=c∣x)p(y = c | \mathbf{x})p(y=c∣x),

fB(x)=arg⁡max⁡c∈Y p(y=c∣x)\begin{equation} f_B(\mathbf{x}) = \underset{c \in \mathbf{Y}}{\arg\max} \ p(y = c | \mathbf{x}) \end{equation}fB​(x)=c∈Yargmax​ p(y=c∣x)​​

But in most real-world scenarios we don’t have p(y∣x)p(y | \mathbf{x})p(y∣x) or any reliable way to estimate it, we only p(y)p(y)p(y) and p(x∣y)p(\mathbf{x} | y)p(x∣y).

So by using Bayes’ rule, the posterior probability can be expressed as,

p(y=c∣x)=p(x∣y=c) p(y=c)p(x)\begin{equation} p(y = c | \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} | y = c) \ p(y = c)}{p(\mathbf{x})} \end{equation}p(y=c∣x)=p(x)p(x∣y=c) p(y=c)​​​

We can see that we have a decision boundary, or where the posterior probabilities meet. We’ll talk more about decision boundaries later.

So, in summary for the Bayes Optimal Classifier,

  • Training
    • Estimate the class conditional densities p(x∣y=c)p(x | y = c)p(x∣y=c) for each class ccc using MLE where distribution is assumed to be Gaussian.
    • Estimate the prior probabilities p(y=c)p(y = c)p(y=c) using MLE.
  • Classification
    • Given a new sample x⋆\mathbf{x}^{\star}x⋆, calculate the probability p(x⋆∣y=c)p(\mathbf{x}^{\star} | y = c)p(x⋆∣y=c) for each class ccc.
    • Pick the class ccc with the largest posterior probability p(y = c | x⋆\mathbf{x}^{\star}x⋆).
      • Equivalently use p(x⋆∣y=c)p(y=c)p(\mathbf{x}^{\star} | y = c) p(y = c)p(x⋆∣y=c)p(y=c).

The Bayes Optimal Classifier is the best classifier in theory, but it is often not practical because it is hard estimating p(x∣y=c)p(\mathbf{x} | y = c)p(x∣y=c).

Naive Bayes Classifier

Technically, the entire previous section is wrong in notation. I’ve used x\mathbf{x}x to denote the feature vector, but the method proposed is for a single feature.

If we have multiple features, the joint probability p(x∣y=c)p(\mathbf{x} | y = c)p(x∣y=c) can’t be modeled as a single Gaussian distribution. So let’s make a naive assumption that the features are statistically independent given the class label, e.g., in 2D,

p(x1,x2∣y=c)=p(x1∣y=c)p(x2∣y=c)\begin{equation} p(x_1, x_2 | y = c) = p(x_1 | y = c) p(x_2 | y = c) \end{equation}p(x1​,x2​∣y=c)=p(x1​∣y=c)p(x2​∣y=c)​​

The general form for classification is,

fNB(x)=arg⁡max⁡c p(y=c)∏j=1Np(xj∣y=c),\begin{equation} f_{NB}(\mathbf{x}) = \underset{c}{\arg\max} \ p(y = c) \prod_{j=1}^{N} p(x_j | y = c), \end{equation}fNB​(x)=cargmax​ p(y=c)j=1∏N​p(xj​∣y=c),​​

where NNN is the number of features.

I will consider the 2D case for visualization and building your intuition, but everything we discuss can be generalized to NNN dimensions.

The only difference from the previous section is that we now have parameters (μj∣c,σj∣c2)(\mu_{j | c}, \sigma_{j | c}^2)(μj∣c​,σj∣c2​) instead of (μc,σc2)(\mu_{c}, \sigma_{c}^2)(μc​,σc2​).

TODO

Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)

So far we have only considered univariate Gaussian distributions. Why not extend this to multivariate Gaussian distributions?

However, LDA makes one assumption, that the covariance matrix is the same for all classes,

p(x∣y=c)=1∣(2π)NΣ∣1/2exp⁡(−12(x−μc)TΣ−1(x−μc)),\begin{equation} p(\mathbf{x} | y = c) = \frac{1}{|(2\pi)^N \Sigma|^{1/2}} \exp \left( -\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \mu_c) \right), \end{equation}p(x∣y=c)=∣(2π)NΣ∣1/21​exp(−21​(x−μc​)TΣ−1(x−μc​)),​​

where Σ\SigmaΣ is the covariance matrix.

As with (Naive) Bayes Classifier, these parameters are learned from the data using MLE.

For now, let’s focus on class conditional densities p(x∣y=c)p(\mathbf{x} | y = c)p(x∣y=c). Let’s look at the log-likelihood function.

(μc^,Σ^)=arg⁡max⁡μc,Σlog⁡[∏1(2π)N/2∣Σ∣1/2exp⁡(−12(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc))]=arg⁡max⁡μc,Σ∑log⁡[1(2π)N/2∣Σ∣1/2exp⁡(−12(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc))]=arg⁡max⁡μc,Σ∑[log⁡(1(2π)N/2∣Σ∣1/2)+log⁡(exp⁡(−12(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc)))] ∣log⁡(ab)=log⁡(a)+log⁡(b)=arg⁡max⁡μc,Σ∑[log⁡(1)⏟0−log⁡((2π)N/2∣Σ∣1/2)+log⁡(exp⁡(−12(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc)))] ∣log⁡(a/b)=log⁡(a)−log⁡(b)=arg⁡max⁡μc,Σ∑[−log⁡((2π)N/2∣Σ∣1/2)−12(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc)] ∣log⁡(ea)=a=arg⁡max⁡μc,Σ∑[−(log⁡((2π)N/2)+log⁡(∣Σ∣1/2))−12(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc)] ∣log⁡(ab)=log⁡(a)+log⁡(b)=arg⁡max⁡μc,Σ∑[−N2log⁡(2π)−12log⁡(∣Σ∣)−12(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc)] ∣log⁡(ab)=blog⁡(a)\begin{aligned} (\hat{\mu_c}, \hat{\Sigma}) & = \underset{\mu_c, \Sigma}{\arg\max} \log \left[\prod \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{N/2} |\Sigma|^{1/2}} \exp \left( -\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) \right) \right] \newline & = \underset{\mu_c, \Sigma}{\arg\max} \sum \log \left[ \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{N/2} |\Sigma|^{1/2}} \exp \left( -\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) \right) \right] \newline & = \underset{\mu_c, \Sigma}{\arg\max} \sum \left[ \log \left( \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{N/2} |\Sigma|^{1/2}} \right) + \log \left( \exp \left( -\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) \right) \right) \right] \ \biggr\rvert \log(ab) = \log(a) + \log(b) \newline & = \underset{\mu_c, \Sigma}{\arg\max} \sum \left[ \underbrace{\log(1)}_0 - \log \left( (2\pi)^{N/2} |\Sigma|^{1/2} \right) + \log \left( \exp \left( -\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) \right) \right) \right] \ \biggr\rvert \log(a/b) = \log(a) - \log(b) \newline & = \underset{\mu_c, \Sigma}{\arg\max} \sum \left[ -\log \left( (2\pi)^{N/2} |\Sigma|^{1/2} \right) - \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) \right] \ \biggr\rvert \log(e^a) = a \newline & = \underset{\mu_c, \Sigma}{\arg\max} \sum \left[ -\left(\log\left((2\pi)^{N/2}\right) + \log\left(|\Sigma|^{1/2}\right) \right) - \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) \right] \ \biggr\rvert \log(ab) = \log(a) + \log(b) \newline & = \underset{\mu_c, \Sigma}{\arg\max} \sum \left[ -\frac{N}{2} \log(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2} \log\left(|\Sigma|\right) - \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) \right] \ \biggr\rvert \log(a^b) = b \log(a) \newline \end{aligned}(μc​^​,Σ^)​=μc​,Σargmax​log[∏(2π)N/2∣Σ∣1/21​exp(−21​(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc​))]=μc​,Σargmax​∑log[(2π)N/2∣Σ∣1/21​exp(−21​(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc​))]=μc​,Σargmax​∑[log((2π)N/2∣Σ∣1/21​)+log(exp(−21​(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc​)))] ​log(ab)=log(a)+log(b)=μc​,Σargmax​∑​0log(1)​​−log((2π)N/2∣Σ∣1/2)+log(exp(−21​(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc​)))​ ​log(a/b)=log(a)−log(b)=μc​,Σargmax​∑[−log((2π)N/2∣Σ∣1/2)−21​(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc​)] ​log(ea)=a=μc​,Σargmax​∑[−(log((2π)N/2)+log(∣Σ∣1/2))−21​(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc​)] ​log(ab)=log(a)+log(b)=μc​,Σargmax​∑[−2N​log(2π)−21​log(∣Σ∣)−21​(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc​)] ​log(ab)=blog(a)​

Then to find the MLE estimates, we differentiate the log-likelihood function with respect to μc\mu_cμc​ and Σ\SigmaΣ and set them to zero.

Let’s start with μc\mu_cμc​.

∂∂μc∑[−N2log⁡(2π)−12log⁡(∣Σ∣)−12(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc)]=0∑∂∂μc[−N2log⁡(2π)⏟0−12log⁡(∣Σ∣)⏟0−12(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc)]=0∑∂∂μc[−12(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc)]=0.\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_c} \sum \left[ -\frac{N}{2} \log(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2} \log\left(|\Sigma|\right) - \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) \right] & = 0 \newline \sum \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_c} \left[ \underbrace{-\frac{N}{2} \log(2\pi)}_0 - \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \log\left(|\Sigma|\right)}_0 - \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) \right] & = 0 \newline \sum \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_c} \left[ -\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) \right] & = 0. \end{aligned}∂μc​∂​∑[−2N​log(2π)−21​log(∣Σ∣)−21​(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc​)]∑∂μc​∂​​0−2N​log(2π)​​−021​log(∣Σ∣)​​−21​(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc​)​∑∂μc​∂​[−21​(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc​)]​=0=0=0.​

Now we need to use the identity ∂∂vvTAv=2Av\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{v}} \mathbf{v}^T A \mathbf{v} = 2 A \mathbf{v}∂v∂​vTAv=2Av, by letting v=x(i)−μc\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_cv=x(i)−μc​ and using the chain rule — where the inner product is just −I-\mathbf{I}−I, we get,

∑∂∂μc[−12(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc)]=0∑122Σ−1(x(i)−μc)=0∑Σ−1(x(i)−μc)=0Σ−1∑(x(i)−μc)=0∑(x(i)−μc)=0 ∣Σ−1 is invertible∑x(i)−Mcμc=0 ∣doesn’t depend on sumμc^=1Mc∑x(i).\begin{aligned} \sum \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_c} \left[ -\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) \right] & = 0 \newline \sum \frac{1}{2} 2 \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) & = 0 \newline \sum \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) & = 0 \newline \Sigma^{-1} \sum (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) & = 0 \newline \sum (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) & = 0 \ \biggr\rvert \Sigma^{-1} \text{ is invertible} \newline \sum \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - M_c \mu_c & = 0 \ \biggr\rvert \text{doesn't depend on sum} \newline \hat{\mu_c} & = \boxed{\frac{1}{M_c} \sum \mathbf{x}^{(i)}}. \end{aligned}∑∂μc​∂​[−21​(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc​)]∑21​2Σ−1(x(i)−μc​)∑Σ−1(x(i)−μc​)Σ−1∑(x(i)−μc​)∑(x(i)−μc​)∑x(i)−Mc​μc​μc​^​​=0=0=0=0=0 ​Σ−1 is invertible=0 ​doesn’t depend on sum=Mc​1​∑x(i)​.​

The mean MLE is the same as the MLE for the univariate Gaussian distribution.

Now for the covariance matrix Σ\SigmaΣ.

∂∂Σ∑[−N2log⁡(2π)−12log⁡(∣Σ∣)−12(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc)]=0∑∂∂Σ[−N2log⁡(2π)⏟0−12log⁡(∣Σ∣)−12(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc)]=0,\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial}{\partial \Sigma} \sum \left[ -\frac{N}{2} \log(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2} \log\left(|\Sigma|\right) - \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) \right] & = 0 \newline \sum \frac{\partial}{\partial \Sigma} \left[ \underbrace{-\frac{N}{2} \log(2\pi)}_0 - \frac{1}{2} \log\left(|\Sigma|\right) - \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) \right] & = 0, \end{aligned}∂Σ∂​∑[−2N​log(2π)−21​log(∣Σ∣)−21​(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc​)]∑∂Σ∂​​0−2N​log(2π)​​−21​log(∣Σ∣)−21​(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc​)​​=0=0,​

From here we need to use two identities — ∂∂Alog⁡∣A∣=(A−1)T\frac{\partial}{\partial A} \log|A| = (A^{-1})^T∂A∂​log∣A∣=(A−1)T and ∂∂AaTA−1b=−A−TabTA−T\frac{\partial}{\partial A} \mathbf{a}^T A^{-1} \mathbf{b} = -A^{-T} \mathbf{a} \mathbf{b}^T A^{-T}∂A∂​aTA−1b=−A−TabTA−T — where the former holds if AAA is symmetric.

Thus,

∑∂∂Σ[−12log⁡(∣Σ∣)−12(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc)]=0∑−12Σ−1+12Σ−1(x(i)−μc)(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1=0∑−Σ−1+Σ−1(x(i)−μc)(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1=0 ∣⋅2∑Σ−1(−I+(x(i)−μc)(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1)=0 ∣left factorize Σ−1Σ−1∑(−I+(x(i)−μc)(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1)=0 ∣doesn’t depend on sum∑(−I+(x(i)−μc)(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1)=0 ∣Σ−1 is invertible−McI+∑(x(i)−μc)(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1=0 ∣I doesn’t depend on sum∑(x(i)−μc)(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1=McI∑(x(i)−μc)(x(i)−μc)TΣ−1Σ=McIΣ ∣right multiply by Σ∑(x(i)−μc)(x(i)−μc)T=McΣΣ=1Mc∑(x(i)−μc)(x(i)−μc)T.\begin{aligned} \sum \frac{\partial}{\partial \Sigma} \left[ -\frac{1}{2} \log\left(|\Sigma|\right) - \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) \right] & = 0 \newline \sum -\frac{1}{2} \Sigma^{-1} + \frac{1}{2} \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} & = 0 \newline \sum -\Sigma^{-1} + \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} & = 0 \ \biggr\rvert \cdot 2 \newline \sum \Sigma^{-1} \left(-\mathbf{I} + (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1}\right) & = 0 \ \biggr\rvert \textbf{left } \text{factorize } \Sigma^{-1} \newline \Sigma^{-1} \sum \left(-\mathbf{I} + (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1}\right) & = 0 \ \biggr\rvert \text{doesn't depend on sum} \newline \sum \left(-\mathbf{I} + (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1}\right) & = 0 \ \biggr\rvert \Sigma^{-1} \text{ is invertible} \newline -M_c \mathbf{I} + \sum (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} & = 0 \ \biggr\rvert \mathbf{I} \text{ doesn't depend on sum} \newline \sum (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} & = M_c \mathbf{I} \newline \sum (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T \Sigma^{-1} \Sigma & = M_c \mathbf{I} \Sigma \ \biggr\rvert \textbf{right } \text{multiply by } \Sigma \newline \sum (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T & = M_c \Sigma \newline \Sigma & = \boxed{\frac{1}{M_c} \sum (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c) (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mu_c)^T}. \end{aligned}∑∂Σ∂​[−21​log(∣Σ∣)−21​(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1(x(i)−μc​)]∑−21​Σ−1+21​Σ−1(x(i)−μc​)(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1∑−Σ−1+Σ−1(x(i)−μc​)(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1∑Σ−1(−I+(x(i)−μc​)(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1)Σ−1∑(−I+(x(i)−μc​)(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1)∑(−I+(x(i)−μc​)(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1)−Mc​I+∑(x(i)−μc​)(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1∑(x(i)−μc​)(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1∑(x(i)−μc​)(x(i)−μc​)TΣ−1Σ∑(x(i)−μc​)(x(i)−μc​)TΣ​=0=0=0 ​⋅2=0 ​left factorize Σ−1=0 ​doesn’t depend on sum=0 ​Σ−1 is invertible=0 ​I doesn’t depend on sum=Mc​I=Mc​IΣ ​right multiply by Σ=Mc​Σ=Mc​1​∑(x(i)−μc​)(x(i)−μc​)T​.​
Summary Generative Models

TODO

I want to emphasize that we’ve taken a more frequentist approach (MLE) to the problem, but one can also take a Bayesian approach 7.

Discriminative Models

We’ve seen that generative models model the joint distribution p(x,y)p(\mathbf{x}, y)p(x,y) — more specifically the class-conditional densities p(x∣y)p(\mathbf{x} | y)p(x∣y) and the prior probabilities p(y)p(y)p(y) — and then use Bayes’ rule to calculate the posterior probabilities p(y∣x)p(y | \mathbf{x})p(y∣x).

But density estimation is hard and an ill-posed problem (to some extent), let’s take a discriminative approach instead, let’s solve for p(y∣x)p(y | \mathbf{x})p(y∣x) directly.

Logistic Regression

Let’s start with the simplest classifier, a binary linear classifier from a logistic regression perspective.

So we have the setup that our observation/feature vector x∈RN\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^Nx∈RN, and we want to predict our class label y∈{−1,+1}y \in \{-1, +1\}y∈{−1,+1}. Note, one can use a zero-indexed class label but using −1-1−1 and +1+1+1 yields a nice property that we will see later on.

Our goal is to have a linear function depends on x\mathbf{x}x that,

f(x)=wTx+b=∑j=1Nwjxj+b,\begin{equation} f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = \sum_{j = 1}^N w_j x_j + b, \end{equation}f(x)=wTx+b=j=1∑N​wj​xj​+b,​​

where w∈RN\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^Nw∈RN is the weight vector used to multiply each feature value and then sum.

From this we decide on a decision rule that is, if f(x)f(\mathbf{x})f(x) then predict class y=1y = 1y=1, if f(x)<0f(\mathbf{x}) < 0f(x)<0 then predict class y=−1y = - 1y=−1. Equivalently, the decision rule is, y=sign(f(x))y = \text{sign}(f(\mathbf{x}))y=sign(f(x)).

This is why we have −1-1−1 and +1+1+1 as class labels.

Our linear classifier will separate the feature space into 2 half-spaces

In a NNN-dimensional feature space x∈RN\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^Nx∈RN our parameters are w∈RN\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^Nw∈RN.

Our equation — wTx+b=0\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0wTx+b=0 defines an N−1N - 1N−1-dimensional (linear) surface. In general, we call this the hyperplane.

But how do we find the parameters (w,b)(\mathbf{w}, b)(w,b)?

Logistic regression takes a probabilistic approach to classification. But there’s a problem, to take a probabilistic approach we need a function to map the value of f(x)=wTx+bf(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + bf(x)=wTx+b to a probability value (between 0 and 1).

Luckily, the sigmoid function maps any real-value to [0,1][0, 1][0,1]. It is defined as,

σ(z)=11+e−z,z∈R\begin{equation} \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad z \in \mathbb{R} \end{equation}σ(z)=1+e−z1​,z∈R​​
Problem: Exercise: What is the derivative of the sigmoid function?
Answer
σ(z)=11+e−zddzσ(z)=ddz(1+e−z)−1=−1⋅(1+e−z)−2⋅−e−z∣ Chain rule=e−z(1+e−z)2=11+e−z⋅e−z1+e−z=σ(z)⋅(1−σ(z))\begin{aligned} \sigma(z) &= \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \newline \frac{d}{dz} \sigma(z) &= \frac{d}{dz} \left(1 + e^{-z}\right)^{-1} \newline &= -1 \cdot \left(1 + e^{-z}\right)^{-2} \cdot -e^{-z} \biggr\rvert \text{ Chain rule} \newline &= \frac{e^{-z}}{\left(1 + e^{-z}\right)^2} \newline &= \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}} \newline &= \sigma(z) \cdot \left(1 - \sigma(z)\right) \end{aligned}σ(z)dzd​σ(z)​=1+e−z1​=dzd​(1+e−z)−1=−1⋅(1+e−z)−2⋅−e−z​ Chain rule=(1+e−z)2e−z​=1+e−z1​⋅1+e−ze−z​=σ(z)⋅(1−σ(z))​

Thus, given a feature vector x\mathbf{x}x the probability of the classes are,

p(y=+1∣x)=σ(f(x))p(y=−1∣x)=1−σ(f(x))=σ(−f(x))\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} p(y = +1 | \mathbf{x}) &= \sigma(f(\mathbf{x})) \newline p(y = -1 | \mathbf{x}) &= 1 - \sigma(f(\mathbf{x})) = \sigma(-f(\mathbf{x})) \end{aligned} \end{equation}p(y=+1∣x)p(y=−1∣x)​=σ(f(x))=1−σ(f(x))=σ(−f(x))​​​

Equivalently, we can write this as,

p(y∣x)=σ(yf(x))\begin{equation} p(y | \mathbf{x}) = \sigma(yf(\mathbf{x})) \end{equation}p(y∣x)=σ(yf(x))​​

[!PROBLEM/Exercise: Can you prove that 1−σ(z)=σ(−z)1 - \sigma(z) = \sigma(-z)1−σ(z)=σ(−z)?]

Answer
1−σ(z)=1−11+e−z ∣⋅1+e−z=1+e−z−11+e−z=e−z1+e−z ∣⋅1e−z=11+ez=σ(−z)\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} 1 - \sigma(z) &= 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \ \biggr\rvert \cdot 1 + e^{-z} \newline &= \frac{1 + e^{-z} - 1}{1 + e^{-z}} \newline &= \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}} \ \biggr\rvert \cdot \frac{1}{e^{-z}} \newline &= \frac{1}{1 + e^{z}} \newline &= \sigma(-z) \end{aligned} \end{equation}1−σ(z)​=1−1+e−z1​ ​⋅1+e−z=1+e−z1+e−z−1​=1+e−ze−z​ ​⋅e−z1​=1+ez1​=σ(−z)​​​

Thus, we are directly modeling the class posterior probability p(y∣x)p(y | \mathbf{x})p(y∣x).

Just like Naive Bayes and LDA we learn the parameters from the data.

By maximizing the (log) likelihood,

(w⋆,b⋆)=arg⁡max⁡w,b∏i=1Np(y(i)∣x(i);w,b)=arg⁡max⁡w,b∏i=1Nσ(y(i)(wTx(i)+b))=arg⁡max⁡w,b∑i=1Nlog⁡σ(y(i)(wTx(i)+b)) ∣log⁡(⋅)\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} (\mathbf{w}^{\star}, b^{\star}) &= \underset{\mathbf{w}, b}{\arg\max} \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(y^{(i)} | \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \mathbf{w}, b) \newline & = \underset{\mathbf{w}, b}{\arg\max} \prod_{i=1}^{N} \sigma\left(y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b)\right) \newline & = \underset{\mathbf{w}, b}{\arg\max} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log \sigma\left(y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b)\right) \ \biggr\rvert \log(\cdot) \newline \end{aligned} \end{equation}(w⋆,b⋆)​=w,bargmax​i=1∏N​p(y(i)∣x(i);w,b)=w,bargmax​i=1∏N​σ(y(i)(wTx(i)+b))=w,bargmax​i=1∑N​logσ(y(i)(wTx(i)+b)) ​log(⋅)​​​

However, this does not have a closed-form solution like our previous models. For now, we will leave it at this, but we will come back and see how we eventually can solve for these parameters.

Support Vector Machines (SVMs)

So far we’ve only looked at probabilistic models, they all use the same probabilistic framework (MLE) to learn how to classify the data.

But when you think about it, a purely geometric approach to classification should be possible and easy to understand. This is what the Support Vector Machine (SVM) does, a purely geometric approach to classification.

For now, let’s assume that our data is linearly separable.

If our data is linearly separable, we will have many possible solutions for a hyperplane. We need somekind of criterion to choose the best hyperplane.

Maximum Margin Principle

Let us define a naive criterion, the maximum margin principle.

We define the distance between the separating line and the closest point as the margin. Intuitively, we think of this space as the amount of “wiggle room” for any potential errors in estimating w\mathbf{w}w.

Thus, we say that the best line is the one that maximizes the margin (i.e., has the most distance between the closest point(s) and the hyperplane).

By symmetry, there should be at least one margin point on each side of the hyperplane (assuming the data is linearly separable).

These points on the margin(s) are called the support vectors (these points define the margin).

Computing the Margin

How do we compute the margin from wTx+b=0\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0wTx+b=0 to a point (x(i),y(i))(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})(x(i),y(i))?

We compute the geometric distance from the point to the hyperplane,

d(i)=y(i)(wTx(i)+b)∥w∥2\begin{equation} d^{(i)} = \frac{y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2} \end{equation}d(i)=∥w∥2​y(i)(wTx(i)+b)​​​

Then we simply compute this distance and take the minimum of all the distances,

d=min⁡{d(i)}i=1M=min⁡(x,y)∈Dy(wTx+b)∥w∥2\begin{equation} d = \min \{d^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{M} = \underset{(\mathbf{x}, y) \in \mathcal{D}}{\min} \frac{y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2} \end{equation}d=min{d(i)}i=1M​=(x,y)∈Dmin​∥w∥2​y(wTx+b)​​​

Thus, the maximum margin is found by solving,

max⁡w,b(min⁡(x,y)∈Dy(wTx+b)∥w∥2)max⁡w,b(1∥w∥2min⁡(x,y)∈Dy(wTx+b))\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \underset{\mathbf{w}, b}{\max} \left(\underset{(\mathbf{x}, y) \in \mathcal{D}}{\min} \frac{y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2}\right) \newline \underset{\mathbf{w}, b}{\max} \left(\frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2} \underset{(\mathbf{x}, y) \in \mathcal{D}}{\min} y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)\right) \newline \end{aligned} \end{equation}w,bmax​((x,y)∈Dmin​∥w∥2​y(wTx+b)​)w,bmax​(∥w∥2​1​(x,y)∈Dmin​y(wTx+b))​​​

[!PROBLEM/Exercise: If we rescale (w,b)(\mathbf{w}, b)(w,b) does our objective function change?]

Answer

No, even if we rescale w→γw\mathbf{w} \to \gamma \mathbf{w}w→γw and b→γbb \to \gamma bb→γb the objective function remains the same.

Why is Maximize the Margin a Good Idea?

Let’s take a step back and think about why maximizing the margin is a good idea.

Firstly, the true w\mathbf{w}w is uncertain (we only have a finite amount of data), maximizing the margin allows the most uncertainty (wiggle room) for w\mathbf{w}w, while keeping all the points on the correct side of the hyperplane. Also, the data is uncertain, maximizing the margin allows the most uncertainty (wiggle room) for the data, while keeping all the points on the correct side of the hyperplane.

Thus, maximizing the margin is a good idea because it allows the most uncertainty for both the data and the true w\mathbf{w}w.

Soft-Margin

So far we have assumed that the data is completely linearly separable, but this is rarely the case in practice.

Let’s now imagine that the data is almost linearly separable, but there are a few points that are on the wrong side of the hyperplane.

How should we proceed? We allow some samples to violate the margin.

We define the slack variable ξi≥0\xi_i \geq 0ξi​≥0 for each sample (x(i),y(i))(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})(x(i),y(i)), where ξi=0\xi_i = 0ξi​=0 means that our sample is outside of margin area (no slack) and ξi>0\xi_i > 0ξi​>0 means our sample is inside the margin area (slack).

(Convex) Optimization

Before we continue, we need to take a detour and talk about (convex) optimization.

A lot of machine learning problems can not be solved analytically and we need to rely on numerical optimization methods to solve them.

But what does this mean? Much of machine learning can be written as an optimization problem,

min⁡θ ℓ(D;θ)=min⁡θ1M∑i=1Mℓ(x(i),y(i);θ)\begin{equation} \underset{\mathbf{\theta}}{\min} \ \ell(\mathcal{D}; \mathbf{\theta}) = \underset{\mathbf{\theta}}{\min} \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i = 1}^M \ell(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}; \mathbf{\theta}) \end{equation}θmin​ ℓ(D;θ)=θmin​M1​i=1∑M​ℓ(x(i),y(i);θ)​​

where θ\mathbf{\theta}θ is our parameter vector to be optimized, ℓ(⋅)\ell(\cdot)ℓ(⋅) is our loss function, and D\mathcal{D}D is our dataset.

Problem: Exercise: What is the loss function for logistic regression?
Answer
ℓ(x(i),y(i);w,b)=log⁡σ(y(i)(wTx(i)+b))=log⁡(11+exp⁡(−y(i)(wTx(i)+b)))=log⁡(1)−log⁡(1+exp⁡(−y(i)(wTx(i)+b)))∣log⁡(a/b)=log⁡(a)−log⁡(b)=−log⁡(1+exp⁡(−y(i)(wTx(i)+b)))∣log⁡(1)=0\begin{aligned} \ell(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}; \mathbf{w}, b) & = \log \sigma(y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b)) \newline & = \log \left(\frac{1}{1 + \exp(-y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b))}\right) \newline & = \log(1) - \log(1 + \exp(-y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b))) \biggr\rvert \log(a/b) = \log(a) - \log(b) \newline & = -\log(1 + \exp(-y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b))) \biggr\rvert \log(1) = 0 \newline \end{aligned}ℓ(x(i),y(i);w,b)​=logσ(y(i)(wTx(i)+b))=log(1+exp(−y(i)(wTx(i)+b))1​)=log(1)−log(1+exp(−y(i)(wTx(i)+b)))​log(a/b)=log(a)−log(b)=−log(1+exp(−y(i)(wTx(i)+b)))​log(1)=0​

When solving optimization problems, it is important to know which type of problem we have.

Convex optimization is the easy case since we can guarantee to find the optimal solution (we will prove this later). Non-convex optimization is the hard case since we can not guarantee to find the optimal solution.

Understanding (convex) optimization is a huge part of (modern) machine learning. But I feel like there a lot of good sources on these … so I will give the brief introduction and explanation, if it is not sufficient find a good source and read about it.

To understand convex optimization, we first need to understand what a convex set is.

Recall that a line segment between two points x(1)\mathbf{x}^{(1)}x(1) and x(2)\mathbf{x}^{(2)}x(2) is defined as,

x=αx(1)+(1−α)x(2),0≤α≤1.\begin{equation} \mathbf{x} = \alpha \mathbf{x}^{(1)} + (1 - \alpha) \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \quad 0 \leq \alpha \leq 1. \end{equation}x=αx(1)+(1−α)x(2),0≤α≤1.​​

A convex set is a set which contains all line segments between any two points in the set,

x(1),x(2)∈χ,0≤α≤1⇒x=αx(1)+(1−α)x(2)∈χ,\begin{equation} \mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)} \in \chi, 0 \leq \alpha \leq 1 \Rightarrow \mathbf{x} = \alpha \mathbf{x}^{(1)} + (1 - \alpha) \mathbf{x}^{(2)} \in \chi, \end{equation}x(1),x(2)∈χ,0≤α≤1⇒x=αx(1)+(1−α)x(2)∈χ,​​

where χ\chiχ is our convex set.

A function, f:RN↦Rf : \mathbb{R}^N \mapsto \mathbb{R}f:RN↦R is convex if dom(f)\text{dom}(f)dom(f) is a convex set and,

f(αx(1)+(1−α)x(2))≤αf(x(1))+(1−α)f(x(2))\begin{equation} f(\alpha \mathbf{x}^{(1)} + (1 - \alpha) \mathbf{x}^{(2)}) \leq \alpha f(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) + (1 - \alpha) f(\mathbf{x}^{(2)}) \end{equation}f(αx(1)+(1−α)x(2))≤αf(x(1))+(1−α)f(x(2))​​

The most simple way one can understand convex functions is, any line segment between two points lies above the curve.

As we mentioned earlier, we can guarantee to find the optimal solution for convex optimization problems. Let’s prove this now, I will use a proof of contradiction.

Proof: Optimal Solution for Convex Optimization

Suppose xxx is a local optimum and yyy is a global optimum with f(y)<f(x)f(y) < f(x)f(y)<f(x). The local optimum xxx implies that there is a radius R>0R > 0R>0 such that,

z∈dom(f),∥z−x∥2≤R⇒f(x)≤f(z).z \in \text{dom}(f), \Vert z - x \Vert_2 \leq R \Rightarrow f(x) \leq f(z).z∈dom(f),∥z−x∥2​≤R⇒f(x)≤f(z).

Now consider z=θy+(1−θ)xz = \theta y + (1 - \theta)xz=θy+(1−θ)x with θ=R2∥y−x∥2\theta = \frac{R}{2 \Vert y - x \Vert_2}θ=2∥y−x∥2​R​. First, we note that ∥y−x∥2>R\Vert y - x \Vert_2 > R∥y−x∥2​>R, due to our assumptions. Therefore, we have 0≤θ≤120 \leq \theta \leq \frac{1}{2}0≤θ≤21​, which implies that zzz is a convex combination of xxx and yyy and is in the domain of fff. Note also that,

∥z−x∥2=∥θy+(1−θ)x−x∥2=∥θ(y−x)∥2=θ∥y−x∥2=R2,\Vert z - x \Vert_2 = \Vert \theta y + (1 - \theta)x - x \Vert_2 = \Vert \theta (y - x) \Vert_2 = \theta \Vert y - x \Vert_2 = \frac{R}{2},∥z−x∥2​=∥θy+(1−θ)x−x∥2​=∥θ(y−x)∥2​=θ∥y−x∥2​=2R​,

which implies that f(x)≤f(z)f(x) \leq f(z)f(x)≤f(z). As fff is convex, we have,

f(z)=f(θy+(1−θ)x)≤θf(y)+(1−θ)f(x)<θf(x)+(1−θ)f(x)=f(x),f(z) = f(\theta y + (1 - \theta)x) \leq \theta f(y) + (1 - \theta) f(x) < \theta f(x) + (1 - \theta) f(x) = f(x),f(z)=f(θy+(1−θ)x)≤θf(y)+(1−θ)f(x)<θf(x)+(1−θ)f(x)=f(x),

which is a contradiction, thus any local optimum must be a global optimum.

Standard Form of Convex Optimization

Convex optimization problems can be written in the following standard form,

min⁡xf0(x)subject tofi(x)≤0,i=1,…,rhi(x)=0,i=1,…,s\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \underset{\mathbf{x}}{\min} & \quad f_0(\mathbf{x}) \newline \text{subject to} & \quad f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, r \newline & \quad h_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, s \end{aligned} \end{equation}xmin​subject to​f0​(x)fi​(x)≤0,i=1,…,rhi​(x)=0,i=1,…,s​​​

The optimal value is,

p⋆=min⁡{f0(x)∣fi(x)≤0,i=1,…,r,hi(x)=0,i=1,…,s}\begin{equation} p^{\star} = \min\{f_0(\mathbf{x}) | f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, i = 1, \ldots, r, h_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0, i = 1, \ldots,s \} \end{equation}p⋆=min{f0​(x)∣fi​(x)≤0,i=1,…,r,hi​(x)=0,i=1,…,s}​​
Duality and KKT Conditions

But, how do we solve these optimization problems?

Let’s first introduce the Lagrangian 8.

We have our standard form optimization problem (not necessarily convex),

min⁡xf0(x)subject tofi(x)≤0,i=1,…,rhi(x)=0,i=1,…,s\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \underset{\mathbf{x}}{\min} & \quad f_0(\mathbf{x}) \newline \text{subject to} & \quad f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, r \newline & \quad h_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, s \end{aligned} \end{equation}xmin​subject to​f0​(x)fi​(x)≤0,i=1,…,rhi​(x)=0,i=1,…,s​​​

With our variable x∈RN\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^Nx∈RN and its domain χ\chiχ and the optimal value p⋆p^{\star}p⋆.

The Lagrangian is defined as,

L(x,λ,ν)=f0(x)+∑i=1rλifi(x)+∑i=1sνihi(x).\begin{equation} L(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{\lambda}, \mathbf{\nu}) = f_0(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{r} \lambda_i f_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{s} \nu_i h_i(\mathbf{x}). \end{equation}L(x,λ,ν)=f0​(x)+i=1∑r​λi​fi​(x)+i=1∑s​νi​hi​(x).​​

The domain dom(L)=χ×Rr×Rs\text{dom}(L) = \chi \times \mathbb{R}^r \times \mathbb{R}^sdom(L)=χ×Rr×Rs. The lagrangian is a weighted sum of the objective function and the constraints. The coefficients λi\lambda_iλi​ and νi\nu_iνi​ are called the Lagrange multipliers.

The lagrangian dual function g:Rr×Rs↦Rg : \mathbb{R}^r \times \mathbb{R}^s \mapsto \mathbb{R}g:Rr×Rs↦R is defined as,

g(λ,ν)=inf⁡x∈χ L(x,λ,ν).\begin{equation} g(\mathbf{\lambda}, \mathbf{\nu}) = \underset{\mathbf{x} \in \chi}{\inf} \ L(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{\lambda}, \mathbf{\nu}). \end{equation}g(λ,ν)=x∈χinf​ L(x,λ,ν).​​

With this we can prove the lower bound property,

Proof: Lower Bound Property

If λ≥0\mathbf{\lambda} \geq 0λ≥0, then g(λ,ν)≤p⋆g(\mathbf{\lambda}, \mathbf{\nu}) \leq p^{\star}g(λ,ν)≤p⋆. Proof: If x~\tilde{x}x~ is feasible and λ≥0\mathbf{\lambda} \geq 0λ≥0, then,

f0(x~)≥L(x~,λ,ν)≥inf⁡x∈χL(x,λ,ν)=g(λ,ν).f_0(\tilde{x}) \geq L(\tilde{x}, \mathbf{\lambda}, \mathbf{\nu}) \geq \underset{\mathbf{x} \in \chi}{\inf} L(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{\lambda}, \mathbf{\nu}) = g(\mathbf{\lambda}, \mathbf{\nu}).f0​(x~)≥L(x~,λ,ν)≥x∈χinf​L(x,λ,ν)=g(λ,ν).

Minimizing over all feasible x~\tilde{x}x~ gives p⋆≥g(λ,ν)p^{\star} \geq g(\mathbf{\lambda}, \mathbf{\nu})p⋆≥g(λ,ν).

Problem: Exercise: What is the lower-bound for the following optimization problem?
min⁡xxTxsubject toAx=b\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \underset{\mathbf{x}}{\min} & \quad \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x} \newline \text{subject to} & \quad \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b} \end{aligned} \end{equation}xmin​subject to​xTxAx=b​​​
Answer

Firstly, rewrite it to standard form,

min⁡xxTxsubject toAx−b=0\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \underset{\mathbf{x}}{\min} & \quad \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x} \newline \text{subject to} & \quad \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} = 0 \end{aligned} \end{equation}xmin​subject to​xTxAx−b=0​​​

Formulate the Lagrangian L(x,ν)=xTx+νT(Ax−b)L(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{\nu}) = \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{\nu}^T (\mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})L(x,ν)=xTx+νT(Ax−b) We take gradient of the Lagrangian and set it equal to zero to, ∇xL(x,ν)=2x+ATν=0\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{\nu}) = 2 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{\nu} = 0∇x​L(x,ν)=2x+ATν=0. Solve for x\mathbf{x}x to get x=−12ATν\mathbf{x} = -\frac{1}{2} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{\nu}x=−21​ATν. Substitute this into the dual function to get g(ν)=−14νTAATν−bTνg(\mathbf{\nu}) = -\frac{1}{4} \mathbf{\nu}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{\nu} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{\nu}g(ν)=−41​νTAATν−bTν. Thus, this is the lower bound for all ν\mathbf{\nu}ν.

Notice how our original problem — which we will call the primal problem — is a minimization problem, while the dual problem is a maximization problem,

max⁡λ,νg(λ,ν)subject toλ⪰0\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \underset{\mathbf{\lambda}, \mathbf{\nu}}{\max} & \quad g(\mathbf{\lambda}, \mathbf{\nu}) \newline \text{subject to} & \quad \mathbf{\lambda} \succeq 0 \end{aligned} \end{equation}λ,νmax​subject to​g(λ,ν)λ⪰0​​​

λ,ν\mathbf{\lambda}, \mathbf{\nu}λ,ν are (dual) feasible if λ⪰0\mathbf{\lambda} \succeq 0λ⪰0 (here ⪰0\succeq 0⪰0 means that every λi≥0\lambda_i \geq 0λi​≥0) and (λ,ν)∈dom(g)(\mathbf{\lambda}, \mathbf{\nu}) \in \text{dom}(g)(λ,ν)∈dom(g).

The dual problem is always a convex optimization problem, and the optimal value of the dual problem is always a lower bound on the optimal value of the primal problem. Yes everything above is in bold because it is important. Really take time to understand why this is important (and powerful).

We denote the optimal value of the dual problem as d⋆d^{\star}d⋆. We say that we have weak duality if d⋆≤p⋆d^{\star} \leq p^{\star}d⋆≤p⋆. This always holds, both for convex and non-convex problems.

The interesting case is strong duality, or d⋆=p⋆d^{\star} = p^{\star}d⋆=p⋆, this usually does not hold in general but usually holds for convex problems (e.g., SVMs, which we will derive later).

Assume that strong duality holds such that x⋆\mathbf{x}^{\star}x⋆ is primal optimal and (λ⋆,ν⋆)(\mathbf{\lambda}^{\star}, \mathbf{\nu}^{\star})(λ⋆,ν⋆) is dual optimal, then,

f(x⋆)=g(λ⋆,ν⋆)=inf⁡x∈χ(f0(x)+∑i=1rλi⋆fi(x)+∑i=1sνi⋆hi(x))≤f0(x⋆)+∑i=1rλi⋆fi(x⋆)+∑i=1sνi⋆hi(x⋆)\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} f(\mathbf{x}^{\star}) = g(\mathbf{\lambda}^{\star}, \mathbf{\nu}^{\star}) & = \underset{\mathbf{x} \in \chi}{\inf} \left(f_0(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{r} \lambda_i^{\star} f_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{s} \nu_i^{\star} h_i(\mathbf{x})\right) \newline & \leq f_0(\mathbf{x}^{\star}) + \sum_{i=1}^{r} \lambda_i^{\star} f_i(\mathbf{x}^{\star}) + \sum_{i=1}^{s} \nu_i^{\star} h_i(\mathbf{x}^{\star}) \newline \end{aligned} \end{equation}f(x⋆)=g(λ⋆,ν⋆)​=x∈χinf​(f0​(x)+i=1∑r​λi⋆​fi​(x)+i=1∑s​νi⋆​hi​(x))≤f0​(x⋆)+i=1∑r​λi⋆​fi​(x⋆)+i=1∑s​νi⋆​hi​(x⋆)​​​

If we let λi⋆fi(x⋆)=0\lambda_i^{\star} f_i(\mathbf{x}^{\star}) = 0λi⋆​fi​(x⋆)=0 for i=1,…,ri = 1, \ldots, ri=1,…,r — which we will call complementary slackness) — then we have,

f(x⋆)=g(λ⋆,ν⋆)=inf⁡x∈χ(f0(x)+∑i=1rλi⋆fi(x)+∑i=1sνi⋆hi(x))≤f0(x⋆)+∑i=1rλi⋆fi(x⋆)+∑i=1sνi⋆hi(x⋆)≤f0(x⋆)\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} f(\mathbf{x}^{\star}) = g(\mathbf{\lambda}^{\star}, \mathbf{\nu}^{\star}) & = \underset{\mathbf{x} \in \chi}{\inf} \left(f_0(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{r} \lambda_i^{\star} f_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{s} \nu_i^{\star} h_i(\mathbf{x})\right) \newline & \leq f_0(\mathbf{x}^{\star}) + \sum_{i=1}^{r} \lambda_i^{\star} f_i(\mathbf{x}^{\star}) + \sum_{i=1}^{s} \nu_i^{\star} h_i(\mathbf{x}^{\star}) \newline & \leq f_0(\mathbf{x}^{\star}) \end{aligned} \end{equation}f(x⋆)=g(λ⋆,ν⋆)​=x∈χinf​(f0​(x)+i=1∑r​λi⋆​fi​(x)+i=1∑s​νi⋆​hi​(x))≤f0​(x⋆)+i=1∑r​λi⋆​fi​(x⋆)+i=1∑s​νi⋆​hi​(x⋆)≤f0​(x⋆)​​​

The two inequalities hold with equality. x⋆\mathbf{x}^{\star}x⋆ not only minimizes f0(x)f_0(\mathbf{x})f0​(x), but also minimizes L(x,λ⋆,ν⋆)L(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{\lambda}^{\star}, \mathbf{\nu}^{\star})L(x,λ⋆,ν⋆).

We’ve finally covered the four KKT (Karush-Kuhn-Tucker) conditions!

  1. Primal constraints: fi(x⋆)≤0f_i(\mathbf{x}^{\star}) \leq 0fi​(x⋆)≤0 for i=1,…,r,hi(x⋆)=0i = 1, \ldots, r, h_i(\mathbf{x}^{\star}) = 0i=1,…,r,hi​(x⋆)=0 for i=1,…,si = 1, \ldots, si=1,…,s.
  2. Dual constraints: λ⋆⪰0\mathbf{\lambda}^{\star} \succeq 0λ⋆⪰0.
  3. Complementary slackness: λi⋆fi(x⋆)=0\lambda_i^{\star} f_i(\mathbf{x}^{\star}) = 0λi⋆​fi​(x⋆)=0 for i=1,…,ri = 1, \ldots, ri=1,…,r.
  4. Gradient of Lagrangian with respect to x\mathbf{x}x vanishes,
∇xL(x⋆,λ⋆,ν⋆)=0\begin{equation} \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L(\mathbf{x}^{\star}, \mathbf{\lambda}^{\star}, \mathbf{\nu}^{\star}) = 0 \end{equation}∇x​L(x⋆,λ⋆,ν⋆)=0​​

If strong duality holds and x⋆\mathbf{x}^{\star}x⋆ are optimal, then they must satisfy the KKT conditions.

SVMs Continued

Let’s now write our hard-margin SVM as an optimization problem,

min⁡w,b12∥w∥22subject toy(i)(wTx(i)+b)≥1,i=1,…,M\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \underset{\mathbf{w}, b}{\min} & \quad \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 \newline \text{subject to} & \quad y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1, \quad i = 1, \ldots, M \end{aligned} \end{equation}w,bmin​subject to​21​∥w∥22​y(i)(wTx(i)+b)≥1,i=1,…,M​​​

Note that our constraint is from the fact that rescaling (w,b)(\mathbf{w}, b)(w,b) does not change the objective function. Therefore, we set the margin to be 111,

min⁡(x,y)∈Dy(wTx+b)=1\begin{equation} \underset{(\mathbf{x}, y) \in \mathcal{D}}{\min} y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b) = 1 \end{equation}(x,y)∈Dmin​y(wTx+b)=1​​

For the soft-margin SVM, we introduced the slack variable ξi≥0\xi_i \geq 0ξi​≥0 for each sample (x(i),y(i))(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})(x(i),y(i)),

min⁡w,b12∥w∥22+C∑i=1Mξisubject toy(i)(wTx(i)+b)≥1−ξi,i=1,…,Mξi≥0,i=1,…,M\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \underset{\mathbf{w}, b}{\min} & \quad \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i \newline \text{subject to} & \quad y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad i = 1, \ldots, M \newline & \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, M \end{aligned} \end{equation}w,bmin​subject to​21​∥w∥22​+Ci=1∑M​ξi​y(i)(wTx(i)+b)≥1−ξi​,i=1,…,Mξi​≥0,i=1,…,M​​​

We also introduce a (hyper)parameter CCC as a penalty for violating the margin (too much slack). Now that we have both the hard-margin and soft-margin SVMs, let’s derive their respective dual problems.

Deriving the Dual Problem

Let’s now derive the dual problem for SVMs, since they are so alike, I will derive both “at the same time”. The unique part(s) of the soft-margin SVM will be highlighted in purple\colorbox{purple}{\text{purple}}purple​.

We have the following optimization problem,

min⁡w,b12∥w∥22+C∑i=1Mξisubject toy(i)(wTx(i)+b)≥1−ξi,i=1,…,Mξi≥0i=1,…,M\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \underset{\mathbf{w}, b}{\min} & \quad \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 + \colorbox{purple}{$C \sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i$} \newline \text{subject to} & \quad y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \geq 1 \colorbox{purple}{$- \xi_i$}, \quad i = 1, \ldots, M \newline & \quad \colorbox{purple}{$\xi_i \geq 0 \quad i = 1, \ldots, M$} \end{aligned} \end{equation}w,bmin​subject to​21​∥w∥22​+C∑i=1M​ξi​​y(i)(wTx(i)+b)≥1−ξi​​,i=1,…,Mξi​≥0i=1,…,M​​​​

Firstly, we write the problem into standard form,

min⁡w,b12∥w∥22+C∑i=1Mξisubject to1−y(i)(wTx(i)+b)+ξi≤0,i=1,…,M−ξi≤0i=1,…,M\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \underset{\mathbf{w}, b}{\min} & \quad \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 + \colorbox{purple}{$C \sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i$} \newline \text{subject to} & \quad 1 - y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \colorbox{purple}{$+ \xi_i$} \leq 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, M \newline & \quad \colorbox{purple}{$-\xi_i \leq 0 \quad i = 1, \ldots, M$} \end{aligned} \end{equation}w,bmin​subject to​21​∥w∥22​+C∑i=1M​ξi​​1−y(i)(wTx(i)+b)+ξi​​≤0,i=1,…,M−ξi​≤0i=1,…,M​​​​

Then, we form the Lagrangian,

L(w,b,λ,μ)=12∥w∥22+C∑i=1Mξi+∑i=1Mλi(1−y(i)(wTx(i)+b)+ξi)−∑i=1Mμiξi=12∥w∥22+∑i=1Mλi−λiy(i)(wTx(i)+b)+∑i=1Mξi(C−λi−μi)=12∥w∥22+∑i=1Mλi−λiy(i)wTx(i)+λiy(i)b+∑i=1Mξi(C−λi−μi)=12∥w∥22−∑i=1Mλiy(i)wTx(i)+∑i=1Mλiy(i)b+∑i=1Mλi+∑i=1Mξi(C−λi−μi).\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} L(\mathbf{w}, b, \mathbf{\lambda}, \colorbox{purple}{$\mathbf{\mu}$}) & = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 + \colorbox{purple}{$C \sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i$} + \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i (1 - y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) \colorbox{purple}{$+ \xi_i$}) - \colorbox{purple}{$\sum_{i=1}^{M} \mu_i \xi_i$} \newline & = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 + \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i - \lambda_i y^{(i)} (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b) + \colorbox{purple}{$\sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i (C - \lambda_i - \mu_i)$} \newline & = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 + \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i - \lambda_i y^{(i)} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + \lambda_i y^{(i)} b + \colorbox{purple}{$\sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i (C - \lambda_i - \mu_i)$} \newline & = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 - \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} b + \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i + \colorbox{purple}{$\sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i (C - \lambda_i - \mu_i)$}. \end{aligned} \end{equation}L(w,b,λ,μ​)​=21​∥w∥22​+C∑i=1M​ξi​​+i=1∑M​λi​(1−y(i)(wTx(i)+b)+ξi​​)−∑i=1M​μi​ξi​​=21​∥w∥22​+i=1∑M​λi​−λi​y(i)(wTx(i)+b)+∑i=1M​ξi​(C−λi​−μi​)​=21​∥w∥22​+i=1∑M​λi​−λi​y(i)wTx(i)+λi​y(i)b+∑i=1M​ξi​(C−λi​−μi​)​=21​∥w∥22​−i=1∑M​λi​y(i)wTx(i)+i=1∑M​λi​y(i)b+i=1∑M​λi​+∑i=1M​ξi​(C−λi​−μi​)​.​​​

The dual function is defined as,

g(λ,μ)=max⁡w,b L(w,b,λ,μ).g(\mathbf{\lambda}, \colorbox{purple}{$\mathbf{\mu}$}) = \underset{\mathbf{w}, b}{\max} \ L(\mathbf{w}, b, \mathbf{\lambda}, \colorbox{purple}{$\mathbf{\mu}$)}.g(λ,μ​)=w,bmax​ L(w,b,λ,μ)​.

To maximize LLL with respect to w\mathbf{w}w, bbb \colorbox{purple}{\xi_i}, we take the partial derivative of w\mathbf{w}w, bbb and \colorbox{purple}{\xi_i} of LLL and set the expression to zero, respectively.

∂L(w,b,λ,ξi)∂w=∂∂w[12∥w∥22−∑i=1Mλiy(i)wTx(i)+∑i=1Mλiy(i)b∑i=1Mλi+∑i=1Mξi(C−λi−μi)]=w−∑i=1Mλiy(i)x(i)=0.\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial L(\mathbf{w}, b, \mathbf{\lambda}, \colorbox{purple}{$\xi_i$})}{\partial \mathbf{w}} & = \frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}} \left[\frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 - \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} b \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i + \colorbox{purple}{$\sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i (C - \lambda_i - \mu_i)$} \right] \newline & = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)} = 0. \end{aligned} \end{equation}∂w∂L(w,b,λ,ξi​​)​​=∂w∂​[21​∥w∥22​−i=1∑M​λi​y(i)wTx(i)+i=1∑M​λi​y(i)bi=1∑M​λi​+∑i=1M​ξi​(C−λi​−μi​)​]=w−i=1∑M​λi​y(i)x(i)=0.​​​ ∂L(w,b,λ,ξi)∂b=∂∂b[12∥w∥22−∑i=1Mλiy(i)wTx(i)+∑i=1Mλiy(i)b∑i=1Mλi+∑i=1Mξi(C−λi−μi)]=∑i=1Mλiy(i)=0.\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial L(\mathbf{w}, b, \mathbf{\lambda}, \colorbox{purple}{$\xi_i$})}{\partial b} & = \frac{\partial}{\partial b} \left[\frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 - \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} b \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i + \colorbox{purple}{$\sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i (C - \lambda_i - \mu_i)$} \right] \newline & = \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} = 0. \end{aligned} \end{equation}∂b∂L(w,b,λ,ξi​​)​​=∂b∂​[21​∥w∥22​−i=1∑M​λi​y(i)wTx(i)+i=1∑M​λi​y(i)bi=1∑M​λi​+∑i=1M​ξi​(C−λi​−μi​)​]=i=1∑M​λi​y(i)=0.​​​ ∂L(w,b,λ,ξi)∂ξi=∂∂ξi[12∥w∥22−∑i=1Mλiy(i)wTx(i)+∑i=1Mλiy(i)b∑i=1Mλi+∑i=1Mξi(C−λi−μi)]=C−λi−μi=0.\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial L(\mathbf{w}, b, \mathbf{\lambda}, \colorbox{purple}{$\xi_i$})}{\colorbox{purple}{$\partial \xi_i$}} & = \frac{\partial}{\colorbox{purple}{$\partial \xi_i$}} \left[\frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 - \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} b \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i + \colorbox{purple}{$\sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i (C - \lambda_i - \mu_i)$} \right] \newline & = \colorbox{purple}{$C - \lambda_i - \mu_i = 0$}. \end{aligned} \end{equation}∂ξi​​∂L(w,b,λ,ξi​​)​​=∂ξi​​∂​[21​∥w∥22​−i=1∑M​λi​y(i)wTx(i)+i=1∑M​λi​y(i)bi=1∑M​λi​+∑i=1M​ξi​(C−λi​−μi​)​]=C−λi​−μi​=0​.​​​

Thus, we can solve for w\mathbf{w}w from the first equation and use the other two as constraints.

w=∑i=1Mλiy(i)x(i).\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}.w=i=1∑M​λi​y(i)x(i).

If we plug back these into our original Lagrangian,

L(w,b,λ,ξi)=12(∑i=1Mλiy(i)x(i))T(∑j=1Mλjy(j)x(j))−∑i=1Mλiy(i)(∑j=1Mλjy(j)x(j))Tx(i)+∑i=1Mλiy(i)b+∑i=1Mλi+∑i=1Mξi(C−λi−μi)=∑i=1Mλi+12∑i=1M∑j=1My(i)y(j)λiλj(x(i))Tx(j)−∑i=1M∑j=1My(i)y(j)λiλj(x(i))Tx(j)=∑i=1Mλi−12∑i=1M∑j=1My(i)y(j)λiλj(x(i))Tx(j)\begin{aligned} L(\mathbf{w}, b, \mathbf{\lambda}, \colorbox{purple}{$\xi_i$}) & = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \right)^T \left( \sum_{j = 1}^M \lambda_j y^{(j)} \mathbf{x}^{(j)} \right) - \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} \left( \sum_{j = 1}^M \lambda_j y^{(j)} \mathbf{x}^{(j)} \right) ^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} b + \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i + \colorbox{purple}{$\sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i (C - \lambda_i - \mu_i)$}\newline & = \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i = 1}^M \sum_{j = 1}^M y^{(i)} y^{(j)} \lambda_i \lambda_j (\mathbf{x}^{(i)})^T \mathbf{x}^{(j)} - \sum_{i = 1}^M \sum_{j = 1}^M y^{(i)} y^{(j)} \lambda_i \lambda_j (\mathbf{x}^{(i)})^T \mathbf{x}^{(j)} \newline & = \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i = 1}^M \sum_{j = 1}^M y^{(i)} y^{(j)} \lambda_i \lambda_j (\mathbf{x}^{(i)})^T \mathbf{x}^{(j)} \end{aligned}L(w,b,λ,ξi​​)​=21​(i=1∑M​λi​y(i)x(i))T(j=1∑M​λj​y(j)x(j))−i=1∑M​λi​y(i)(j=1∑M​λj​y(j)x(j))Tx(i)+i=1∑M​λi​y(i)b+i=1∑M​λi​+∑i=1M​ξi​(C−λi​−μi​)​=i=1∑M​λi​+21​i=1∑M​j=1∑M​y(i)y(j)λi​λj​(x(i))Tx(j)−i=1∑M​j=1∑M​y(i)y(j)λi​λj​(x(i))Tx(j)=i=1∑M​λi​−21​i=1∑M​j=1∑M​y(i)y(j)λi​λj​(x(i))Tx(j)​

Note that I have rewritten ∥w∥22\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_{2}^2∥w∥22​ as wTw\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w}wTw.

So, our dual function is,

g(λ)=∑i=1Mλi−12∑i=1M∑j=1My(i)y(j)λiλj(x(i))Tx(j).g(\mathbf{\lambda}) = \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i = 1}^M \sum_{j = 1}^M y^{(i)} y^{(j)} \lambda_i \lambda_j (\mathbf{x}^{(i)})^T \mathbf{x}^{(j)}.g(λ)=i=1∑M​λi​−21​i=1∑M​j=1∑M​y(i)y(j)λi​λj​(x(i))Tx(j).

Combining this and our constraints, we get the following dual optimization problem,

max⁡λ∑i=1Mλi−12∑i=1M∑j=1My(i)y(j)λiλj(x(i))Tx(j).subject to∑i=1Mλiy(i)=0C−λi−μi=0i=1,…,Mλi≥0,i=1,…,Mμi≥0i=1,…,M\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \underset{\lambda}{\max} & \quad \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i = 1}^M \sum_{j = 1}^M y^{(i)} y^{(j)} \lambda_i \lambda_j (\mathbf{x}^{(i)})^T \mathbf{x}^{(j)}. \newline \text{subject to} & \quad \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} = 0 \newline & \quad \colorbox{purple}{$C - \lambda_i - \mu_i = 0$} \quad i = 1, \ldots, M \newline & \quad \lambda_i \geq 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, M \newline & \quad \colorbox{purple}{$\mu_i \geq 0$} \quad i = 1, \ldots, M \end{aligned} \end{equation}λmax​subject to​i=1∑M​λi​−21​i=1∑M​j=1∑M​y(i)y(j)λi​λj​(x(i))Tx(j).i=1∑M​λi​y(i)=0C−λi​−μi​=0​i=1,…,Mλi​≥0,i=1,…,Mμi​≥0​i=1,…,M​​​

Which we can easily rewrite as,

max⁡λ∑i=1Mλi−12∑i=1M∑j=1My(i)y(j)λiλj(x(i))Tx(j).subject to∑i=1Mλiy(i)=00≤λi≤Ci=1,…,M\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \underset{\lambda}{\max} & \quad \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i = 1}^M \sum_{j = 1}^M y^{(i)} y^{(j)} \lambda_i \lambda_j (\mathbf{x}^{(i)})^T \mathbf{x}^{(j)}. \newline \text{subject to} & \quad \sum_{i = 1}^M \lambda_i y^{(i)} = 0 \newline & \quad 0 \leq \lambda_i \colorbox{purple}{$\leq C$} \quad i = 1, \ldots, M \end{aligned} \end{equation}λmax​subject to​i=1∑M​λi​−21​i=1∑M​j=1∑M​y(i)y(j)λi​λj​(x(i))Tx(j).i=1∑M​λi​y(i)=00≤λi​≤C​i=1,…,M​​​

We can see that the only difference lies in the constraint on λi\lambda_iλi​.

In the hard margin case, we do not have an upper-bound, this is due to the ξi\xi_iξi​ term (slackness term), we get the upper-bound CCC. With hard margins, we assume the data is perfectly separable, i.e., no mistakes in classification.

We can think of the hard margin case as a special case of the soft margin, if we let C→∞C \to \inftyC→∞ we get the hard margin case.

However, I lied earlier, SVMs do not have a truly closed form solution, only a quadratic programming/optimization problem 9. These optimization problems are well studied and methods to solve them exists but beyond our scope [^11].

Gradient Descent and variants

But there is another way to find the best parameters, that we also can use for logistic regression.

Gradient descent is an optimization algorithm used to minimize some function by iteratively moving in the direction of steepest descent as defined by the negative of the gradient,

θ=θ−α∇ℓ(θ),\begin{equation} \mathbf{\theta} = \mathbf{\theta} - \alpha \nabla \ell(\mathbf{\theta}), \end{equation}θ=θ−α∇ℓ(θ),​​

where α\alphaα is the learning rate and ∇ℓ(θ)\nabla \ell(\mathbf{\theta})∇ℓ(θ) is the gradient of the loss function evaluated at θ\mathbf{\theta}θ.

Gradient descent picks an initial point θ(0)\mathbf{\theta}^{(0)}θ(0) and iteratively performs,

θ(t+1)=θ(t)−α∇ℓ(D;θ(t))=θ(t)−α1M∑i=1M∇ℓ(x(i),y(i);θ(t)).\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathbf{\theta}^{(t+1)} & = \mathbf{\theta}^{(t)} - \alpha \nabla \ell(\mathcal{D}; \mathbf{\theta}^{(t)}) \newline & = \mathbf{\theta}^{(t)} - \alpha \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \nabla \ell(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}; \mathbf{\theta}^{(t)}). \end{aligned} \end{equation}θ(t+1)​=θ(t)−α∇ℓ(D;θ(t))=θ(t)−αM1​i=1∑M​∇ℓ(x(i),y(i);θ(t)).​​​

But when do we stop?

We can stop when ∥θ(t+1)−θ(t)∥2≤ϵ\Vert \mathbf{\theta}^{(t+1)} - \mathbf{\theta}^{(t)} \Vert_2 \leq \epsilon∥θ(t+1)−θ(t)∥2​≤ϵ for some small ϵ\epsilonϵ. Intuitively, this means that we are not moving much anymore, i.e., we are close to (some) optimum.

Similarly, we can stop if ∥∇ℓ(D;θ(t))∥2≤ϵ\Vert \nabla \ell(\mathcal{D}; \mathbf{\theta}^{(t)}) \Vert_2 \leq \epsilon∥∇ℓ(D;θ(t))∥2​≤ϵ. Again, this can intuitively be interpreted as we are not moving much anymore.

Our initial point matters a lot, as viewed in Figure 12. Therefore, we can run gradient descent multiple times with different initial points and pick the best one.

Our learning rate matters too of course, if we choose a too big α\alphaα, gradient descent might move too quickly and risk never converging. If we choose a too small α\alphaα, gradient descent might take too long to converge.

However, there is a drawback to gradient descent, the computational cost. For each iteration, we need to compute the gradient for all samples, this can be very expensive for large datasets.

Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) is a variant of gradient descent that only uses a (random) single sample to compute the gradient. At the ttt-th iteration, we randomly sample a single input-output pair and compute the gradient,

θ(t+1)=θ(t)−α∇ℓ(x(i),y(i);θ(t)).\begin{equation} \mathbf{\theta}^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{\theta}^{(t)} - \alpha \nabla \ell(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}; \mathbf{\theta}^{(t)}). \end{equation}θ(t+1)=θ(t)−α∇ℓ(x(i),y(i);θ(t)).​​
Problem: Exercise: What is the expected value of SGD over all samples?
Answer

The expected value of SGD over all samples is the same as the gradient descent,

E[ℓ(x,y;θ(t+1))]=1M∑i=1Mℓ(x(i),y(i);θ(t+1))=∇ℓ(D;θ(t)).\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}[\ell(\mathbf{x}, y; \mathbf{\theta}^{(t+1)})] & = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \ell(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}; \mathbf{\theta}^{(t+1)}) \newline & = \nabla \ell(\mathcal{D}; \mathbf{\theta}^{(t)}). \end{aligned} \end{equation}E[ℓ(x,y;θ(t+1))]​=M1​i=1∑M​ℓ(x(i),y(i);θ(t+1))=∇ℓ(D;θ(t)).​​​

Mini-batch gradient descent is a compromise between gradient descent and SGD. At the ttt-th iteration, we randomly sample a mini-batch B⊂D\mathcal{B} \subset \mathcal{D}B⊂D and compute the gradient,

θ(t+1)=θ(t)−α∇ℓ(B;θ(t))=θ(t)−α1∣B∣∑(x(i),y(i))∈B∇ℓ(x(i),y(i);θ(t)).\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathbf{\theta}^{(t+1)} & = \mathbf{\theta}^{(t)} - \alpha \nabla \ell(\mathcal{B}; \mathbf{\theta}^{(t)}) \newline & = \mathbf{\theta}^{(t)} - \alpha \frac{1}{|\mathcal{B}|} \sum_{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}) \in \mathcal{B}} \nabla \ell(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)}; \mathbf{\theta}^{(t)}). \end{aligned} \end{equation}θ(t+1)​=θ(t)−α∇ℓ(B;θ(t))=θ(t)−α∣B∣1​(x(i),y(i))∈B∑​∇ℓ(x(i),y(i);θ(t)).​​​

Let’s now derive the gradient descent update rule for logistic regression. Remember that the loss function for logistic regression is,

ℓ(x,y;w,b)=−log⁡(1+exp⁡(−y(wTx+b))).\begin{equation} \ell(\mathbf{x}, y; \mathbf{w}, b) = -\log(1 + \exp(-y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b))). \end{equation}ℓ(x,y;w,b)=−log(1+exp(−y(wTx+b))).​​

We can rewrite our MLE problem (which has no closed-form solution) as an optimization problem,

max⁡w,b∑i=1M−log⁡(1+exp⁡(−y(i)(wTx(i)+b)))min⁡w,b∑i=1Mlog⁡(1+exp⁡(−y(i)(wTx(i)+b))).\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \underset{\mathbf{w}, b}{\max} & \quad \sum_{i=1}^{M} -\log(1 + \exp(-y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b))) \newline \underset{\mathbf{w}, b}{\min} & \quad \sum_{i=1}^{M} \log(1 + \exp(-y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b))). \end{aligned} \end{equation}w,bmax​w,bmin​​i=1∑M​−log(1+exp(−y(i)(wTx(i)+b)))i=1∑M​log(1+exp(−y(i)(wTx(i)+b))).​​​

Simply using the negative sign to convert the maximization problem to a minimization problem.

Let’s see how the gradient behaves for a single training example (x,y)(\mathbf{x}, y)(x,y). The partial derivative of the loss function with respect to the jjj-th component wjw_jwj​,

∂ℓ(x,y;w,b)∂wj=∂∂wj[−log⁡(σ(y(wTx+b)))]=−1σ(y(wTx+b))∂∂wjσ(y(wTx+b))∣ Chain rule=−1σ(y(wTx+b))σ(y(wTx+b))(1−σ(y(wTx+b)))∂∂wjy(wTx+b)∣ Chain rule=(σ(y(wTx+b))−1)∂∂wjy(wTx+b)=(σ(y(wTx+b))−1)y∂∂wj(wTx+b)=(σ(y(wTx+b))−1)yxj.\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial \ell(\mathbf{x}, y; \mathbf{w}, b)}{\partial w_j} & = \frac{\partial}{\partial w_j} \left[-\log(\sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)))\right] \newline & = -\frac{1}{\sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b))} \frac{\partial}{\partial w_j} \sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)) \biggr\rvert \text{ Chain rule} \newline & = -\frac{1}{\sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b))} \sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)) (1 - \sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b))) \frac{\partial}{\partial w_j} y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b) \biggr\rvert \text{ Chain rule} \newline & = (\sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)) - 1) \frac{\partial}{\partial w_j} y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b) \newline & = (\sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)) - 1) y \frac{\partial}{\partial w_j} (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b) \newline & = (\sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)) - 1) y x_j. \end{aligned}∂wj​∂ℓ(x,y;w,b)​​=∂wj​∂​[−log(σ(y(wTx+b)))]=−σ(y(wTx+b))1​∂wj​∂​σ(y(wTx+b))​ Chain rule=−σ(y(wTx+b))1​σ(y(wTx+b))(1−σ(y(wTx+b)))∂wj​∂​y(wTx+b)​ Chain rule=(σ(y(wTx+b))−1)∂wj​∂​y(wTx+b)=(σ(y(wTx+b))−1)y∂wj​∂​(wTx+b)=(σ(y(wTx+b))−1)yxj​.​

The partial derivative of the loss function with respect to bbb,

∂ℓ(x,y;w,b)∂b=∂∂b[−log⁡(σ(y(wTx+b)))]=−1σ(y(wTx+b))∂∂bσ(y(wTx+b)) Chain rule=−1σ(y(wTx+b))σ(y(wTx+b))(1−σ(y(wTx+b)))∂∂by(wTx+b) Chain rule=(σ(y(wTx+b))−1)y∂∂b(wTx+b)=(σ(y(wTx+b))−1)y.\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial \ell(\mathbf{x}, y; \mathbf{w}, b)}{\partial b} & = \frac{\partial}{\partial b} \left[-\log(\sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)))\right] \newline & = -\frac{1}{\sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b))} \frac{\partial}{\partial b} \sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)) \text{ Chain rule} \newline & = -\frac{1}{\sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b))} \sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)) (1 - \sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b))) \frac{\partial}{\partial b} y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b) \text{ Chain rule} \newline & = (\sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)) - 1) y \frac{\partial}{\partial b} (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b) \newline & = (\sigma(y(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)) - 1) y. \end{aligned}∂b∂ℓ(x,y;w,b)​​=∂b∂​[−log(σ(y(wTx+b)))]=−σ(y(wTx+b))1​∂b∂​σ(y(wTx+b)) Chain rule=−σ(y(wTx+b))1​σ(y(wTx+b))(1−σ(y(wTx+b)))∂b∂​y(wTx+b) Chain rule=(σ(y(wTx+b))−1)y∂b∂​(wTx+b)=(σ(y(wTx+b))−1)y.​

Thus, the (vectorized) gradient descent update rules for logistic regression are,

w(t+1)=w(t)−α1M∑i=1M(σ(y(i)(w(t)Tx(i)+b(t))−1)y(i)x(i)b(t+1)=b(t)−α1M∑i=1M(σ(y(i)(w(t)Tx(i)+b(t))−1)y(i).\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathbf{w}^{(t+1)} & = \mathbf{w}^{(t)} - \alpha \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} (\sigma(y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{(t)T} \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b^{(t)}) - 1) y^{(i)} \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \newline b^{(t+1)} & = b^{(t)} - \alpha \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} (\sigma(y^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}^{(t)T} \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b^{(t)}) - 1) y^{(i)}. \end{aligned} \end{equation}w(t+1)b(t+1)​=w(t)−αM1​i=1∑M​(σ(y(i)(w(t)Tx(i)+b(t))−1)y(i)x(i)=b(t)−αM1​i=1∑M​(σ(y(i)(w(t)Tx(i)+b(t))−1)y(i).​​​
kkk-Nearest Neighbors

Well, this detour to derive the dual problem for SVMs was quite long along with understanding gradient descent has taken quite a bit of time. But let’s introduce another geometric approach to classification, kkk-Nearest Neighbors (KNN).

The idea is to store all the data D={(x(i),y(i))}i=1M\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{M}D={(x(i),y(i))}i=1M​ and when we want to classify a new point x\mathbf{x}x we use a majority vote over its kkk-nearest neighbors Nk(x)⊂D\mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}) \subset \mathcal{D}Nk​(x)⊂D. KNNs therefore require a distance function d:X×X→Rd : \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}d:X×X→R and the number of neighbors kkk.

Therefore, the classification function is,

fKNN(x)=argmaxy∈Y∑i∈Nk(x)I[y(i)=y],\begin{equation} f_{\text{KNN}}(\mathbf{x}) = \underset{y \in \mathcal{Y}}{\text{argmax}} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x})} \mathbb{I}[y^{(i)} = y], \end{equation}fKNN​(x)=y∈Yargmax​i∈Nk​(x)∑​I[y(i)=y],​​

where I\mathbb{I}I is the indicator function.

(Brute force) KNNs work by computing the distance di=d(x(i),x)d_i = d(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, \mathbf{x})di​=d(x(i),x) from the target point x\mathbf{x}x to all other points in the dataset x(i)\mathbf{x}^{(i)}x(i). We then sort the distances {di,i=1,…,M}\{d_i, i = 1, \ldots, M\}{di​,i=1,…,M} and take the kkk-nearest neighbors to create the set Nk(x)\mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x})Nk​(x).

The Regression Task

Okay, we are finally done with classification, but what if our target variable is continuous?

Definition: Definition: The regression task

Given a feature vector x∈X=RN\mathbf{x} \in \mathbf{X} = \mathbb{R}^Nx∈X=RN, predict its corresponding output value y∈Y=Ry \in \mathbf{Y} = \mathbb{R}y∈Y=R.

Let’s take a moment to think about the difference between classification and regression.

In classification, we need to find a hyperplane that separates the classes (data) from each other. While in regression we need to find a function that best fits the data.

While curve fitting is not the same as regression, it is a good analogy. Given MMM points from some underlying function (assuming it exists and has negligible noise) our goal is to find this function.

Problem: Exercise: Here are four questions we need to answer to understand curve fitting (and by extension regression),
  1. Does a small subset of points work, or is the more data the better?
  2. Do the locations of the points (on the curve) matter?
  3. Given the same set of MMM points, is the curve(s) unique?
  4. If (3) is false, how do we measure the quality of the curve(s)?
Answer
  1. More data is better, as it gives us a better approximation of the underlying function.
